items.title: BREAKING: Global LNG exports fell -7% MoM in April, to 33 million tons, the lowest since May 2024. This marks the 3rd consecutive monthly decline. Sin...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Global LNG exports fell -7% MoM in April, to 33 million tons, the lowest since May 2024.

This marks the 3rd consecutive monthly decline.

Since the January peak, LNG exports have dropped -9 million tons or -21%.

This collapse was driven by Qatar, the world's 2nd-largest LNG exporter, halting production after Iranian strikes destroyed its largest plant in March, with damage estimated to take years to repair.

The damage to Qatar’s LNG infrastructure is set to delay the anticipated global LNG supply expansion wave by at least 2 years, with a cumulative loss of ~120 billion cubic meters of LNG supply projected between 2026 and 2030.

All the while the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed despite the ceasefire.

We are facing an unprecedented energy crisis.

items.title: Global airlines are cancelling flights at an unprecedented pace: Airlines have cut 2 million seats and 12,000 flights worldwide from their May schedul...
items.description_html:
Global airlines are cancelling flights at an unprecedented pace:

Airlines have cut 2 million seats and 12,000 flights worldwide from their May schedules over the last 2 weeks, reducing the total available seats to 130 million.

This comes as jet fuel costs have DOUBLED since the Iran war began, forcing carriers to cancel unprofitable routes, switch to smaller aircraft, and raise ticket prices.

Turkish Airlines and Air China account for the largest seat reductions, cutting ~520,000 and ~490,000 seats, respectively.

Lufthansa leads in flight cancellations, at ~4,000 flights in May alone, with the airline having removed 20,000 flights from its schedule between May and October.

Meanwhile, Gulf carriers, including Emirates, Etihad, and Qatar Airways, are still operating well below pre-conflict capacity, as the closure of Gulf airports has disrupted ~33% of all European journeys to Asia.

Singapore and Tokyo airports asked carriers not to add extra services to limit jet fuel use, and Vietnam introduced jet fuel rationing.

The global aviation shock is spreading.

items.title: AI is driving consumer energy costs higher: The RGGI carbon price, which covers CO2 emissions from power plants across 10 northeastern US states, jump...
items.description_html:
AI is driving consumer energy costs higher:

The RGGI carbon price, which covers CO2 emissions from power plants across 10 northeastern US states, jumped +31% last week, to $47 per metric ton, the highest in at least 4 years.

This increase briefly pushed RGGI prices past California's record CO2 price of $44 set in 2024.

In other words, AI-driven power demand in the Northeast is repricing carbon risk faster than California's broader economy-wide market, with RGGI having historically traded at a fraction of California's price.

This comes amid anticipation of Virginia's planned return to the market in July which will sharply increase demand for carbon permits, as this state contains a large amount of AI data centers.

This is adding further pressure to consumer electricity bills, which are already rising due to the AI-driven boom in power demand.

The AI buildout is redefining energy markets.

items.title: BREAKING: The Nasdaq 100 ETF, $QQQ, has attracted +$10 billion in inflows over the last 21 days, the highest 21-day total since October 2025. This is ...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The Nasdaq 100 ETF, $QQQ, has attracted +$10 billion in inflows over the last 21 days, the highest 21-day total since October 2025.

This is also the 3rd-largest 21-day inflow on record.

This follows -$11 billion in outflows recorded in March, the highest in at least 4 years.

US-listed ETFs posted +$174 billion in inflows in April, more than any other region.

Year-to-date, US-listed ETFs have pulled in a massive +$673 billion.

The top-performing fund was the S&P 500 ETF, $VOO, which attracted +$25 billion in April and +$47 billion year-to-date.

Investors are buying equity funds at a rapid pace.

items.title: BREAKING: Global central bank gold purchases surged +36 tonnes in Q1 2026, to 244 tonnes, the highest since Q4 2024. This significantly exceeds the 5-...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Global central bank gold purchases surged +36 tonnes in Q1 2026, to 244 tonnes, the highest since Q4 2024.

This significantly exceeds the 5-year average of ~228 tonnes.

By comparison, the 5-year average in 2016-2020 was ~115 tonnes, or less than half the current level.

Central banks have now bought over +200 tonnes of gold in 10 out of the last 11 quarters.

Poland led Q1 purchases at +31 tonnes, bringing its total reserves to a record 582 tonnes, heading closer to its 700 tonnes target.

Uzbekistan followed at +25 tonnes and China at +7 tonnes.

World central banks are acquiring gold at an accelerated pace.

items.title: The world is using more US Dollars than ever: Offshore US Dollar deposits held in banks outside the US are up to a record ~$14.5 trillion. This is +22...
items.description_html:
The world is using more US Dollars than ever:

Offshore US Dollar deposits held in banks outside the US are up to a record ~$14.5 trillion.

This is +220% more than the ~$4.5 trillion held at the beginning of the century.

By comparison, only ~$3.5 trillion worth of euros are held in offshore banks, outside the Eurozone.

And, the Fed and domestic commercial banks hold over $19.0 trillion.

This means offshore US Dollar deposits are now equivalent to~43% of US domestic bank deposits, with no other currency coming close to this percentage.

Global demand for USD is incredibly high.

items.title: BREAKING: The ratio of US leading to coincident economic indicators is now down to 0.84, matching the 2008 Financial Crisis low. This comes as the Lea...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The ratio of US leading to coincident economic indicators is now down to 0.84, matching the 2008 Financial Crisis low.

This comes as the Leading Economic Index (LEI) fell -0.6% MoM in March, posting its 7th monthly decline out of the last 8.

The Conference Board Leading Economic Index (LEI) tracks forward-looking data, including consumer expectations, manufacturing orders, weekly hours, and initial jobless claims.

At the same time, the Coincident Economic Index (CEI) measures current economic conditions in real time, such as nonfarm payrolls or personal income, excluding government social security or unemployment payments.

This ratio is now on track for its 5th consecutive annual decline, the longest streak on record.

In the past, such depressed levels have never occurred outside of a recession.

The US economy and the stock market are moving in opposite directions.

items.title: Software companies are flooding the US private credit industry: Software loans account for ~20% of all loans deployed by Business Development Companie...
items.description_html:
Software companies are flooding the US private credit industry:

Software loans account for ~20% of all loans deployed by Business Development Companies (BDCs).

BDCs are publicly traded firms that lend to small, mid-sized, and distressed US businesses, offering retail investors access to private credit markets.

Software exposure has now DOUBLED since 2016.

By comparison, Commercial Services accounts for ~19%, with Commercial Products and Healthcare Services representing just ~9% and ~6%, respectively.

Furthermore, private credit funds classify some software loans under different sector labels, meaning true exposure could be significantly higher.

This makes it difficult for investors to accurately assess the amount of risk concentrated in a single sector.

Private credit exposure is becoming highly concentrated in software.

items.title: The US auto loan crisis is accelerating: The average amount owed by underwater car borrowers rose to ~$7,200 in Q1 2026, the highest on record and the...
items.description_html:
The US auto loan crisis is accelerating:

The average amount owed by underwater car borrowers rose to ~$7,200 in Q1 2026, the highest on record and the 4th consecutive annual increase.

Over the last 4 years, the average amount owed by negative equity car borrowers has risen +71%.

Overall, ~30% of car buyers who traded in a vehicle in Q1 had negative equity.

This comes as pandemic-era vehicles, bought at peak prices, have lost value faster than borrowers can pay down the loans.

This compounds existing pressure on auto buyers amid elevated vehicle prices and interest rates.

Auto credit stress is spreading.

items.title: BREAKING: President Trump says Fed Chair Powell is a "disaster for America" and that interest rates are "too high." The 10Y Note Yield is nearing 4.50...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: President Trump says Fed Chair Powell is a "disaster for America" and that interest rates are "too high."

The 10Y Note Yield is nearing 4.50% with the average rate for a new 30Y mortgage now above 6.5%.

items.title: Shocking stat of the day: Adjusted Stablecoin volume is projected to reach $719 TRILLION by 2035, according to a new report from Chainalysis. This pro...
items.description_html:
Shocking stat of the day:

Adjusted Stablecoin volume is projected to reach $719 TRILLION by 2035, according to a new report from Chainalysis.

This projection is assuming organic growth alone. When factoring in macro catalysts, Chainalysis says this figure could approach $1.5 quadrillion.

In fact, Stablecoin payment volumes are on pace to match Visa and Mastercard’s off-chain transaction volumes somewhere between 2031 and 2039.

This comes amid a massive shift toward Stablecoin infrastructure, with Western Union, $USDPT, Fidelity, $FIDD, Meta, and many other Fortune 500 companies preparing launches.

The ecosystem is also evolving, such as Jupiter's $JUPUSD, which returns yield back to the ecosystem and has been attracting large inflows.

Stablecoin adoption is skyrocketing.

items.description_html:
RT Shereen Bhan
Top highlights of my conversation with ITC’s Sanjeev Puri
1. Demand not impacted yet, but raw material inflation rising. Lower than the 8-10% impact that HUL spoke of.
2. Some price hikes have been taken in the FMCG business, more likely.
3. Tax impact on cigarettes business significant, typically see a volume Degrowth when taxes are hiked. Have taken a 50-60 % hike in prices, staggering the rest.
4. 20,000 cr capex over the next 4-5 years
5. Big bet on the agri biz, broken even in year 3
6. Hotel biz seen a hit because of West Asia conflict, starting to improve now. Will add a hotel a month, expect domestic demand to drive growth
7. Working towards our target of being India’s number 1 FMCG company
More below 👇 @CNBCTV18News @CNBCTV18Live #CNBCTV18Market

items.description_html:
Super El Nino ?

NY Times (today) “Forecasters say a powerful El Niño weather pattern could form later this year, with a chance of becoming one of the strongest in three decades. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration says there’s a roughly 60% chance of an El Niño developing between May and July.”

CNA Singapore (today) “Last week, the United Nations' weather & climate agency said El Nino conditions could develop as soon as May to July.

The World Meteorological Organization - meanwhile, said early signs indicated the event could be particularly strong, with some dubbing the impending event a "super El Nino"

If true, not great news for most Asian countries !

items.title: RT Camus: Brain scans are revealing early dementia-like changes in kids and teens from heavy screen use. 60 Minutes Australia reported toddlers spendi...
items.description_html:
RT Camus
Brain scans are revealing early dementia-like changes in kids and teens from heavy screen use.

60 Minutes Australia reported toddlers spending just 2–3 hours daily on devices already show abnormal white matter development. Teens averaging 6–8 hours display widened brain ridges and thinning in key areas — patterns that mirror early Alzheimer’s.

Excessive screens appear to weaken neural pathways that normally strengthen through real-world movement, play, and face-to-face interaction.

We’re also seeing the first IQ drops in recorded history, plus a nearly 400% rise in early-onset dementia signs among 35–44 year olds. Correlation, not proven causation — but devices are the major new variable.

This is one of those reports that makes you rethink default habits. The convenience of screens is undeniable, but the potential long-term brain impacts on developing kids are hard to ignore.

We may be unintentionally running a massive experiment on the next generation’s cognitive health.

Are we underestimating the risks of heavy screen time, or is this concern overblown?

items.title: Mahindra & Mahindra – Q4 FY26 summary Strong earnings across Auto, Farm, Mahindra Finance and the growth businesses, with ROE at 20% and management c...
items.description_html:
Mahindra & Mahindra – Q4 FY26 summary

Strong earnings across Auto, Farm, Mahindra Finance and the growth businesses, with ROE at 20% and management calling FY26 a year of continued strong execution.

Business-wise breakdown

Auto: FY26 PAT grew 33% YoY to ₹7,842 crore. SUV volume grew 19% YoY to 1,118k, SUV revenue market share rose to 25.3% (+260 bps YoY), and LCV market share improved to 52.3%. Auto business margin improved to 10.9% in Q4 FY26. 100% PLI compliant

Farm: FY26 PAT grew 13% YoY to ₹4,298 crore. Domestic tractor volumes grew 24% YoY to 526k, market share improved to 43.6% (+30 bps YoY), and farm standalone PBIT margin rose to 19.8% in FY26.

Mahindra Finance: PAT grew 60% YoY to ₹2,855 crore; AUM grew 12% YoY to ₹1,34,000 crore, and GS3 improved to 3.41%. 99% secured portfolio. Udaan-important tech-platform for disbursals.

Tech Mahindra: PAT grew 14% YoY to ₹4,811 crore. TCV was $3,794 million and EBIT margin improved to 12.6% (+290 bps YoY), showing margin-led recovery.

Growth gems: Strong momentum in real estate, logistics, aerostructures, last mile mobility, hospitality and trucks & buses. 7x PAT move in Lifespaces and improving scale in logistics / EV / aero.

Key guidance & management commentary

Management is guiding for mid-single-digit industry growth in Auto & Farm, but expects Mahindra to do better because of product strength, market share gains and capacity additions. 15–18% SUV growth is possible if capacity ramps as planned.

Auto margins are expected to stay healthy, though the mix includes eSUV contract manufacturing which dilutes reported margin. Auto standalone margin was 10.4% for FY26. 10+ new launches in SUV and LCV by FY31

In Farm, the big focus is the new tractor range and product refreshes. (with transmission & power). Exited Sampo, Erkunt business units.

AI is now being treated as a real operating lever, with management citing ₹4,100 crore+ revenue potential in FY27 from AI-led adoption, alongside shorter time-to-market (10% reduced).

The strongest combination is market-share gains + margin expansion + high ROE. Auto is still the key earnings engine, Farm is stable with good margins, Finance is scaling with improving asset quality, and TechM is adding margin-led support.

Auto growth is backed by SUV leadership and EV traction, going to be less supply and more demand. No impact because of fuel prices fluctuations.
Farm is backed by new product cycles,
financial services improving with cleaner asset quality and scale.

items.title: Royal Enfield 350cc: 1-month waiting period. TVS iQube EV: can only meet 50% of demand. Both raised prices in April. Zero demand destruction. My take:...
items.description_html:
Royal Enfield 350cc: 1-month waiting period.

TVS iQube EV: can only meet 50% of demand.

Both raised prices in April. Zero demand destruction.

My take: India just crossed the income threshold where people stop buying what they need and start buying what they want.

China hit $2,500 per capita GDP in 2007.

Durables grew 20%+ annually for the next decade after that.

India crossed $2,500 per capita in FY25-26.

The dealer data is telling you what the quarterly results haven't yet.

Waiting lists and supply constraints in premium products = the thesis is working.

Not a stock call. Just an observation on where we are in the cycle.

items.title: Oil was $70 in February. It's $108 today. That's +54% in 9 weeks. Most people think this is a "crude oil problem." It's not. It's an everywhere proble...
items.description_html:
Oil was $70 in February. It's $108 today.

That's +54% in 9 weeks.
Most people think this is a "crude oil problem." It's not. It's an everywhere problem.
Here's how it actually hits you 👇

- ATF (aviation fuel) is crude-linked.
- IndiGo just cut international capacity by 17% for May.
- When airlines shrink capacity, ticket prices go up.
- When ticket prices go up, discretionary travel drops.
- When travel drops, hotels, tourism, retail all feel it.

And this is just ONE transmission channel. Steel, cement, freight, fertiliser — all move with crude.

The market is treating this like a commodity story. It's a margin story for almost every sector in India.

This is what warflation looks like. Not stagflation. Not a recession. A supply shock that hasn't finished transmitting yet.

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: MS on PNB UW, TP Rs 88 Core revenues were 5% below MSe. Core PPOP adjusted for reversal of retirement provisions (due to higher bond y...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
MS on PNB
UW, TP Rs 88
Core revenues were 5% below MSe.
Core PPOP adjusted for reversal of retirement provisions (due to higher bond yields) also missed MSe
Reversal of retirement provisions, lower credit costs (owing to certain lumpy reversals) & bad loan recoveries drove a strong headline PAT beat.

CLSA on PNB
O-P, TP Rs 135
PNB reported an optical PAT beat of 16% driven by lower-than-expected Opex and credit costs, despite a 5% miss on total income
Key disappointment was NII, which declined 3% QoQ despite growth in its loan book.
Loan growth of 13.7% YoY was slightly below our estimate of 15% YoY.
Deposit growth of 9% YoY was in line with last quarter, but clearly a slowdown from FY25
CASA ratio of 36% was steady which is a decent outcome.
Asset quality performance was decent with the gross slippage ratio declining 20bps YoY to 0.9%
Cut FY27-28CL PAT estimates by 3% on account of lower NII

Jefferies on PNB
Buy, TP Rs 130
Q4 profit of Rs52bn (+14% YoY) was ahead of est, as reversal of retirement benefits & write-back of provisions compensated for weaker NII.
Loan growth improved to 14% YoY, but NIM compression dragged NII.
Headroom in LDR allows PNB to grow loans despite soft deposit growth of 9%.
Slippages fell YoY & core credit costs are controlled.
Ability to sustain ROA despite transition to ECL will be key; valuations reasonable at 0.8x PB.
@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh

items.title: RT menaka doshi: From the Kotak Institutional Equities - Strategy Note by Sanjeev Prasad, MD & Co-Head. "With no major state election scheduled until ...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
From the Kotak Institutional Equities - Strategy Note by Sanjeev Prasad, MD & Co-Head.

"With no major state election scheduled until early 2027, the government enters a relatively election-free corridor of 10 months.

We expect the government to firmly focus on managing India's weakening macro environment, amid
(1) elevated crude oil prices,
(2) risks to food inflation from a sub-par monsoon and
(3) a widening current account deficit.

In our view, policymakers may focus on
(1) rationalizing energy subsidies, (2) accelerating trade diversification,
(3) finalizing the India-US Bilateral Trade Agreement and
(4) accelerating certain slow-moving reforms."

NIM expanded ~13 bps QoQ — the highest amongst the top 4 PVBs — while credit costs normalized to 39 bps (-25 bps YoY) on the back of broad-based asset quality improvement.
Loan growth held up at ~16% YoY, and stable opex growth helped offset weaker NOI to drive RoA back above 2%.
RoE at 12.3%, however, remained below the other large PVBs, weighed down by elevated capital buffers.

UBS on Kotak BK
Buy, TP Rs 500
Q4 PAT beat driven by higher PPOP and lower credit cost
Loan/Deposit growth at 3.2%/5.5% QoQ; CASA ratio up 200bp QoQ
Management expect one time ECL transition impact at <2% of net worth
At current valuations (1.5x March 2027E P/BV vs 1.5x - 1.9x for private peers), risk reward looks favourable given expected acceleration in PPOP growth, improvement in asset quality, range bound NIMs & momentum in non-lending business

JPM on Kotak BK
OW, TP Rs 476
delivered a steady 4Q with PAT at Rs 40.3bn (+13% y/y, RoE: 12.1%) coming largely in-line with JPMe.
Core performance was strong with broad-based beats across NII (+1% vs. JPMe), Opex (-3% vs. JPMe) and loan loss provisions (-35% vs JPMe) partially offset by muted non-interest income (-2% y/y, on unwinding of NDF positions and muted card growth).
NII growth of 8.1% y/y (+4.1% q/q) was among the best in the large private bank peer group, with core NIMs (4.54%) holding steady q/q on deposit re-pricing.
Reported NIMs rose by 13bps q/q to 4.67%, on day count benefit in 4Q.

Nomura on Kotak BK
Buy, TP Rs 460
4Q26: Strong NIM delivery, cautious guidance
Sharp asset quality improvement
Raise FY27-28F EPS by ~2%, led by lower credit costs and opex
Expect RoA/RoE to improve to 2%/12% by FY28F (vs. 1.9%/11% in FY26), with strong EPS CAGR of 18% over FY27-28F

Jefferies on Kotak BK
Buy, TP Rs 450
Q4 standalone profit of Rs40bn (13% YoY) was ahead of est with lower credit costs.
Loans grew by 16% and NII by 8%; growth in CASA was healthy at 16%.
Credit quality is improving with better trends in MFI & credit cards.
Still trim earnings by 3-4% as factor in lower NIM guidance due to higher cost of deposits
Vals are reasonable at 14x FY27E standalone adj. PE.
@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh

items.title: Do you buy the business or do you buy the perception of it ? A question that becomes very important to think about We all judge companies on the basis...
items.description_html:
Do you buy the business or do you buy the perception of it ? A question that becomes very important to think about

We all judge companies on the basis of the business model but also try to see how the market perceives it , by market I mean other participants , a high growth weak management in a bull market business creates the illusion of perpetuity in growth , and a low growth great business creates the perception of deep value

In both cases you may be right/wrong , but what is important is to step back and think about using perception as an advantage

When I think of a company that has growth , rate of change , expanding profit pools with tailwinds , I think of a very important factor called perception shift multiple

Can the perception shift cause the underlying multiples to help you gain excess return in a mediocre business model also , this is the part of unseen returns

Bad cycle : Asset heavy , leveraged , Risky business in a good cycle : Asset backed earnings, de leveraging theme and de risking the book

I always think about can my thesis be a story that can be sold at a higher multiple with elements of optionality that are unseen merely due to the variables of perception shift , and trust me perception shifts reward big time !

Goal is to systematically make a framework that enables ideas as a % of PF that have the perception shift angle which also usually re rates the multiples for excess return !

items.title: Commodity costs were rising sharply, but Ather still improved margins. EBITDA moved from -23.3% to -2.5% over two years (~2080 bps YoY improvement), e...
items.description_html:
Commodity costs were rising sharply, but Ather still improved margins.

EBITDA moved from -23.3% to -2.5% over two years (~2080 bps YoY improvement), even as:
– Aluminium & metals prices rose ~41%
– Battery and electronics costs remained volatile

What helped:
• Better sourcing & inventory planning
• Use of alternate materials (light rare-earth magnets)
• Shift to LFP batteries
• Product design changes

A good example of how execution can offset cost pressures.

Q4FY26

FY26

Operating leverage continues to play out

The key highlight was continued Sector outperformance

SJS delivered 41% YoY growth in automotive business versus 19% YoY growth for the underlying Industry in Q4FY26

For FY26, delivered 35% growth in the 2W + PV segment versus 11% industry growth, meaning the company grew more than 3x the industry

Growth was led by strong traction in both 2W & PV segments.

Q4FY26 segment growth
• 2W segment 🔼41%
• PV segment 🔼41%
• Domestic business 🔼26%
• Exports 🔼75% - 26 Cr

Exports formed around 10% of Q4FY26 REV and continue to scale well

Margin expansion was driven by better product mix, higher export contribution & operational efficiency

Continues to benefit from premiumisation & new age aesthetic products

Company is expanding into higher value products like optical plastics, display systems, illuminated logos, IMD, IML & IME products.

FY27 outlook remains strong
Management expects SJS to continue outperforming the underlying industry growth by 1.5-2x supported by:
• Premiumisation
• Building mega OEM accounts
• Export growth
• New customer additions
• New product pipeline
• Higher kit value per vehicle
• Capacity expansion

Current order book to be executed in FY27 is already over 85% of FY27 forecasted REV

One very interesting framework they shared:
Lending is like sitting on a three legged stool.

The job is to manage 3 costs:
Cost of funds, Cost of operations & Cost of credit

This is such a simple but powerful way to understand lending businesses

Overall, SBFC concalls are always worth reading

items.title: The Real Moat in Lending/Commodity Business⏬ "We sit on a three legged stool, and our principal job is to manage three costs – cost of funds, cost o...
items.description_html:
The Real Moat in Lending/Commodity Business⏬

"We sit on a three legged stool, and our principal job is to manage three costs – cost of funds, cost of operations and cost of credit. Lending is a commodity business, and we are all price takers, not price setters. The most foundational truth of any commodity business from steel, cement, power to finance – the lowest cost producer wins."

~ SBFC Q4 transcript

items.title: Last 5 years have been extremely tough for the Chemical Sector De-stocking, weak demand, pricing pressure, global oversupply etc almost everything wen...
items.description_html:
Last 5 years have been extremely tough for the Chemical Sector

De-stocking, weak demand, pricing pressure, global oversupply etc almost everything went against the sector

Result - Many stocks are down 50-70% from their ATH

There was clear exuberance in 2021, that excess has now been rationalised

The easy China+1 narrative is already behind us.

But niche specialty chemical players with differentiated products, contract manufacturing, CRAMS, pharma intermediates, fluorochemicals, electronic chemicals, battery materials etc can see a better cycle.

So yes, the next 5 years can be much better than the last 5 years
But unlike 2021, this will not be a blind rally in every chemical stock

Do share which companies are worth studying at this point, which you think are sitting at an inflection point⏬

items.description_html:
RT Flexible Capital ⭐
Using a PMS for MFs is like hiring Einstein to teach grade 8 physics

The tax angle isn't the point: the investor directly and via PMS wrapper pays taxes whenever funds are being switched. It's actually the double payment for advisory fee: on the PMS and on the MF (via TER)

A PMS makes sense for direct stocks esp if the manager can outperform constantly (net of fees). Otherwise an RIA or even a good MFD can do the job if you want to stick to MFs

items.title: RT Dhirendra Kumar: Re The pitch reads well. The math underneath does not. The PMS fee sits on top of the mutual fund expense ratio, not in place of i...
items.description_html:
RT Dhirendra Kumar
Re The pitch reads well. The math underneath does not.
The PMS fee sits on top of the mutual fund expense ratio, not in place of it. All-in: 1.1 to 2%. A direct MF portfolio does the same job at 0.5 to 1%.
Tax pass-through is a feature of mutual funds, not the PMS wrapper. The moment the PMS rebalances between schemes, the demat books a redemption and tax follows.
Profit sharing without a hurdle rate is not alignment. The manager keeps 20% of the upside and bears none of the downside. Investors pay carry for beating zero.
A HNI with 50 lakh can buy 4 to 6 direct funds, pay an RIA a flat fee, and keep most of what the wrapper would have taken. The structure pays the provider better. Not the investor.

items.title: RT DepressedBergman: Satyajit Ray on how audience from other countries can connect with his movies: "There are obvious risks in exposing an audience t...
items.description_html:
RT DepressedBergman
Satyajit Ray on how audience from other countries can connect with his movies:

"There are obvious risks in exposing an audience to a wholly alien culture. There was an American lady, for instance, who was so upset by the spectacle of Indians eating with their fingers that she had to leave the theatre as soon as the second dinner episode commenced. But such squeamishness is not common. If details confuse, or offend, or go over the head, there is always the human element to fall back upon.

In 'Apur Sansar' (1959), Apu attends the wedding of a friend’s sister. The bridegroom (this is an arranged marriage) arrives and is found to be touched in the head. With the bride’s mother refusing to give away her daughter, the marriage seems on the verge of being abandoned. If it doesn’t take place at the appointed hour, the bride would be cursed and made unmarriageable for life. The crisis is averted at the last moment by Apu offering to step in as the groom. A Western viewer ignorant of orthodox Hindu customs must find the episode highly bizarre. But since Apu himself finds it so, and since his action is prompted by compassion, the viewer accepts it on moral grounds, though given no opportunity to weigh the pros and cons of a seemingly irrational practice.

Faced with a film from an alien culture, swamped by details which evoke no responses in him, a viewer will often find that the only aspect which offers a common ground is the moral one. Broad concepts of good and evil, right and wrong, do not vary much from culture to culture. Human behaviour also falls within patterns which are largely familiar and predictable. Abnormal situations may affect such behaviour as, say, in Hitler’s Germany; but as long as a film makes the context clear, a viewer is unlikely to lose his bearings. This can happen only when the development of the story itself turns on social and historical factors of which the viewer has no knowledge."

("Deep Focus- Reflections on Cinema", Satyajit Ray, 2011)

P.S: On this day, 67 years ago, 'Apur Sansar' was released in India.

IT services major Cognizant is weighing a reduction in workforce that could impact 12,000-15,000 employees worldwide, with the bulk of the layoffs expected in India, multiple sources told Moneycontrol.

On April 29, the Nasdaq-listed Cognizant said it expects to incur between $230 million and $320 million in severance costs under its newly announced Project Leap, without disclosing the number of potential job losses.

The company employs more than 357,000 people, with most, over 250,000, based in India.

The layoffs estimate is based on back of the envelope calculations using typical salary and severance assumptions across geographies.

In India, the average annual salary is assumed to be around Rs 15 lakh.

With severance ranging between four to six months, the cost per employee rises to roughly $50,000, meaning fewer employees can be impacted for the same level of provisioning.

Combining these assumptions across regions yields a global estimate in the range of 12,000 to 15,000 employees.

Sources said these calculations are indicative and based on broad assumptions around salary, tenure, and geographic mix, and the final number could vary depending on how the restructuring is executed.

With @shaw_reshab

https://www.moneycontrol.com/news/business/information-technology/cognizant-may-cut-12000-15000-jobs-globally-bulk-of-cuts-in-india-13908373.html

items.title: RT Normal Guy: India reserves sit at $698+ billion but that number hides a lot. Gold is $131 billion of that, you cannot sell gold fast to pay for cru...
items.description_html:
RT Normal Guy
India reserves sit at $698+ billion but that number hides a lot.

Gold is $131 billion of that, you cannot sell gold fast to pay for crude imports. RBI forward book is now $104 billion in net short dollar positions, a record.

Strip both out and usable foreign currency drops to around $449 billion. That is roughly where India was in 2021.

The Iran war did not create a reserves problem. It exposed that the real liquid buffer was much thinner than the top-line number

FCNR redux sounds reasonable but the maths is completely different from 2013.

Back then US rates were near zero, so RBI could offer concessional swaps cheaply and still pull in $26 billion.

Today the Fed funds rate is 3.5-3.75%, making every hedged dollar far more expensive for RBI to absorb.
FCNR-B flows have already collapsed 87% this year to under $1 billion.

Even if RBI reopens the window, the cost per dollar raised will eat into its own balance sheet.

Import cover as a metric assumes exports go to zero. The net BoP deficit of $30 billion against $698 billion in reserves gives you a 5% drawdown rate per year, which is manageable in isolation.

The problem is three things hitting at once - crude above $110, FPI outflows crossing $20 billion in 2026, and the rupee already down 5.5% year-to-date.

Each one drains dollars through a different channel simultaneously.


Ira Dugal: India’s central bank is studying ways to boost dollar flows, including the now-famed FCNR route.

While headline level of FX reserves looks adequate, adjusted for gold and forward book, they provide about 7-odd months of import cover.

The thinking is on. Reuters @kalrajs23 and

items.title: RT Tijori: The company is Ajinomoto Co. (TYO: 2802), and the product that matters here is Ajinomoto Build-up Film, or ABF. What makes this interesting...
items.description_html:
RT Tijori
The company is Ajinomoto Co. (TYO: 2802), and the product that matters here is Ajinomoto Build-up Film, or ABF.

What makes this interesting is the switching-cost moat. ABF is a thermosetting film that has to maintain sub-micron flatness across a 600mm sheet, and every single layer needs to be defect-free because one flaw means you scrap the entire 20-layer substrate carrying a $40,000 GPU die. Ajinomoto spent three decades dialing in the exact resin-filler chemistry, controls both the IP and the precursor amino-acid feedstock, and qualifying a new film with TSMC, Intel, or Samsung takes five to seven years of joint testing. So even if Sekisui or a Chinese competitor cracked the chemistry tomorrow, no chipmaker is going to risk a yield disaster just to switch suppliers.

The cleaner play here isn't actually Ajinomoto itself — the food business still accounts for roughly 70% of revenue, which dilutes the AI thesis and is exactly why Palliser has been pushing for a spin-off. If you want pure-play exposure, look downstream at the ABF substrate manufacturers: Unimicron, Nan Ya PCB, Kinsus, Ibiden, Shinko Electric, and AT&S.


SemiAnalysis: There is a single thin-film material required by every AI chip on earth. GPUs, TPUs, custom ASICs. All of them. 98% of global supply controlled by one Japanese chemical company. Zero production-ready alternatives. One producer fully booked through 2027. Raising prices. Lead times

items.title: FSL delivered a strong FY26 with revenue of ₹96,161 million, growing 20.62% YoY and beating management's guidance of 14.5-15.5% by over 5 percentage ...
items.description_html:
FSL delivered a strong FY26 with revenue of ₹96,161 million, growing 20.62% YoY and beating management's guidance of 14.5-15.5% by over 5 percentage points. PAT grew 13.45% to ₹6,744 million with EBITDA margin expanding 97 bps to 16.29%.

Operating cash flow was exceptional at ₹12,140 million (161% of PAT), demonstrating superior earnings quality. Management raised FY27 guidance for revenue growth of 10-13% and EBIT margin of 12.25-12.75%, indicating continued confidence in margin expansion trajectory despite moderating top-line growth.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 20.62% YoY to ₹96,161.20 million, beating management's FY26 guidance of 14.5-15.5% CC growth

PAT grew 13.45% YoY to ₹6,744.25 million; Q4 PAT grew 27.73% YoY

EBITDA margin expanded 97 bps YoY to 16.29% for FY26

Operating cash flow was exceptional at ₹12,139.76 million, up 73.15% YoY, with 161% cash conversion ratio

Banking and Financial Services segment EBIT margin expanded 305 bps to 19.58%

Diverse Industries segment grew 58.7% YoY, fastest growing segment

FY27 guidance raised: Revenue 10-13% CC growth, EBIT margin 12.25-12.75%

Risk Factors

Other expenses grew 35.6% YoY, outpacing revenue growth of 19.75%

Attrition rate increased to 29.7% in Q4 from 27.4% in Q3, reversing positive trend

Trade receivables grew 23.3% YoY, faster than revenue growth of 19.8%, indicating working capital stretch

Short-term borrowings surged 46.8% to ₹17,478.01 million to fund acquisitions and working capital

FY27 revenue guidance of 10-13% represents moderation from 19.75% growth in FY26

items.title: GRWRHITECH Q4 FY26 showed strong recovery with revenue up 8.9% YoY and PAT up 39% YoY, driven by margin expansion of 409 bps to 26.2%. FY26 EBITDA mar...
items.description_html:
GRWRHITECH Q4 FY26 showed strong recovery with revenue up 8.9% YoY and PAT up 39% YoY, driven by margin expansion of 409 bps to 26.2%. FY26 EBITDA margin of 23.6% met management's guided range.

The company generated positive FCF despite elevated capex and maintains a strong balance sheet with ₹744 Cr liquidity. New ₹191 Cr SCF capex and TPU plant commissioning in FY27 provide near-term catalysts.

Positive Factors

Q4 FY26 revenue grew 8.9% YoY to ₹596.69 Cr with 30.1% QoQ recovery

PAT surged 39.1% YoY to ₹108.21 Cr in Q4

EBITDA margin expanded 409 bps YoY to 26.2% in Q4
FY26 EBITDA margin of 23.6% met management's guided range of 22-25%

Generated positive FCF of ₹113.92 Cr despite ₹161.41
Cr capex

Net worth grew 12% YoY to ₹2,656 Cr with liquidity surplus of ₹744.72 Cr

Board approved ₹191 Cr capex for new SCF line (29% capacity addition)

Dividend of ₹12 per share (120%) announced

Risk Factors

FY26 revenue grew only 0.5% YoY to ₹2,120 Cr, missing original guidance of ₹2,500 Cr (withdrawn mid-year)

Raw material cost as % of revenue increased from 46.1% to 50.8% YoY in Q4

Operating cash flow declined to ₹275.33 Cr from ₹329.72 Cr in FY25 due to inventory build-up

US tariff headwinds impacted full year performance

items.title: KPIT's Q3 FY26 results showed significant margin pressure with EBITDA margin breaching the 21% guidance to 20.6% and PAT declining 21.1% QoQ due to a ...
items.description_html:
KPIT's Q3 FY26 results showed significant margin pressure with EBITDA margin breaching the 21% guidance to 20.6% and PAT declining 21.1% QoQ due to a one-time labor code impact of ₹597.12 Cr. Revenue growth decelerated sharply to flat YoY from 15% in Q4 FY25, reflecting client budget pressures.

The strategic acquisition of Cymotive Technologies for automotive cybersecurity and continued dividend payout (₹5.25/share) provide some forward-looking support, but near-term execution remains challenged by macro headwinds and acquisition integration costs.

Positive Factors

Acquisitions (Caresoft, N-Dream) contributed 3.4% to QoQ growth in Q3 FY26

Strategic acquisition of Cymotive Technologies
strengthens automotive cybersecurity capabilities

Final dividend of ₹5.25 per share (52.5%) demonstrates shareholder return commitment

Net cash position of ₹9,000 Cr as of Q3 FY26 indicates strong balance sheet

Acquisition structured with performance-linked consideration (USD 60-120 million) to align incentives

Risk Factors

One-time labor code impact of ₹597.12 Cr directly hit Q3 profitability

EBITDA margin breached 21% guidance, falling to 20.6% in Q3

PAT declined 21.1% QoQ to ₹1,334.10 Cr due to one-off costs and operational pressure

YoY revenue growth decelerated to Flat in Q3 from 15% in Q4 FY25

Client budget pressures and slower deal conversions impacting organic growth

Cymotive's turnover declining: USD 42.4M (CY23) to USD 19.2M (CY25)

items.title: Capillary delivered strong FY26 results with revenue growth of 22.8% meeting its 20%+ target, and normalized PAT growth of 106.5% YoY excluding the on...
items.description_html:
Capillary delivered strong FY26 results with revenue growth of 22.8% meeting its 20%+ target, and normalized PAT growth of 106.5% YoY excluding the one-time ₹249.6 million exceptional gain. The company achieved a transformational turnaround in operating cash flow (₹1,499 million vs negative ₹462 million in FY25) with an exceptional 1.61x CFO-to-EBITDA conversion.

Balance sheet strengthened significantly post-IPO with debt reduced 55% and strong net cash position. The upcoming SessionM acquisition and improving client concentration (43% to 38%) provide near-term catalysts, though margin pressure from rising employee and software costs warrants monitoring.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 22.8% YoY to ₹7,345.99 million (FY26), meeting the 20%+ long-term target

Normalized PAT grew 106.5% YoY to ₹274.28 million excluding one-time exceptional gain

Operating cash flow turned positive at ₹1,499.07 million vs negative ₹461.99 million in FY25

CFO to Normalized EBITDA ratio exceptional at 1.61x

Top 5 customer concentration improved from 43% to 38%, reducing concentration risk

Total debt reduced 55.3% YoY to ₹447.21 million with strong net cash position

Acquired SessionM from Mastercard for USD 20 million post-FY26, key future growth driver

Risk Factors

EBITDA margin contracted 169 bps YoY to 16.41% in Q4 due to higher employee (34.6% YoY) and software
costs (44.2% YoY)

Amortization expense grew 24.7% YoY to ₹749.71 million, depressing reported PAT

Working capital: Trade receivables grew 12.2% YoY while revenue grew 22.8% YoY - collections may need
monitoring

Integration risk from Kognitiv acquisition (11 months) and upcoming SessionM integration

items.title: Arvind delivered a strong Q4 with revenue growth of 14.8% YoY meeting its 12-15% guidance, and turned profitable with PAT of ₹46.97 Cr from a loss. E...
items.description_html:
Arvind delivered a strong Q4 with revenue growth of 14.8% YoY meeting its 12-15% guidance, and turned profitable with PAT of ₹46.97 Cr from a loss. EBITDA grew 17% YoY exceeding the >15% guidance, though margin expansion of 35 bps missed the 50-100 bps target. Online B2C channels grew 45% YoY demonstrating successful D2C execution.

However, net debt doubled to ₹467 Cr and operating cash flow declined 24% due to inventory build-up, creating working capital pressure. The company met most guidance targets but margin underperformance and debt build-up are key concerns.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 14.8% YoY to ₹1,364.79 Cr, meeting guidance of 12-15%

EBITDA grew 17.0% YoY for FY26, exceeding >15% guidance

PAT turned positive at ₹46.97 Cr from loss of ₹93.15 Cr in Q4 FY25

Normalized PAT grew 63.5% YoY to ₹139 Cr for FY26

EBITDA margin expanded 51 bps YoY in Q4 to 13.85%

Online B2C grew 45% YoY, Retail LTL growth stable at 7.8%

ROCE improved to 23.5% from >20% level, exceeding guidance

Pre-IndAS 116 EBITDA grew 21.3% YoY, showing strong underlying performance

Risk Factors

EBITDA margin expansion of 35 bps missed guidance of 50-100 bps

Net debt doubled YoY to ₹467.43 Cr, goal of net cash by FY27 appears challenging

Operating cash flow declined 23.6% YoY due to inventory build-up

Inventories rose 28.7% YoY, outpacing revenue growth of 14%

Working capital days increased significantly, tying up cash

Finance costs rose 9.4% YoY to ₹170.45 Cr

items.title: KPI Green Energy delivered strong FY26 results with 55.3% revenue growth and 35.6% EBITDA margin beating guidance by ~360 bps. The company achieved it...
items.description_html:
KPI Green Energy delivered strong FY26 results with 55.3% revenue growth and 35.6% EBITDA margin beating guidance by ~360 bps. The company achieved its 1 GW IPP capacity target and entered BESS with a 445 MW project.

However, aggressive debt-funded capex (₹3,526 Cr) resulted in significantly negative FCF of ₹-3,102 Cr and Debt Equity ratio tripling to 1.49x, creating financial risk despite strong operational execution.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 55.3% YoY to ₹2,696 Cr, slightly below 60% target but very strong

EBITDA margin of 35.6% exceeded 30-32% guidance by ~360 bps

PAT grew 56.6% YoY to ₹509 Cr, EPS at ₹24.13 up 48.7%

Achieved 1 GW energised IPP capacity target for FY26

Q4 EBITDA margin at 36.7% showed 715 bps YoY expansion

Secured ₹979 Cr debt from Canara Bank for 150 MW wind IPP project

Entered BESS business with 445 MW / 890 MWh project

Operating cash flow doubled to ₹424 Cr

Risk Factors

Debt increased 309% YoY to ₹4,532 Cr, Debt Equity ratio jumped from 0.46 to 1.49

Interest coverage declined from 7.31x to 5.52x due to 213.7% rise in finance costs

FCF significantly negative at ₹-3,102 Cr due to ₹3,526 Cr capex

Working capital days increased with inventory turnover at 247.3 days

ROE of 16.1% missed ~20% guidance for H2 FY26

Current ratio declined from 2.42 to 1.64

items.title: Aeroflex delivered strong Q4 FY26 with 37.24% YoY revenue growth and 57.01% PAT growth, driven by robust demand execution. EBITDA margin expanded 317 ...
items.description_html:
Aeroflex delivered strong Q4 FY26 with 37.24% YoY revenue growth and 57.01% PAT growth, driven by robust demand execution. EBITDA margin expanded 317 bps YoY to 23.75%, and the company met its revised margin outlook of 22-23%.

However, original margin guidance of +100 bps was missed (actual +58 bps), and high capex resulted in negative free cash flow. Outstanding regulatory tax demands of ₹48.95 Cr remain a monitorable risk.

Positive Factors

Q4 FY26 revenue grew 37.24% YoY to ₹12,583.79 lakhs

Q4 PAT grew 57.01% YoY to ₹1,763.50 lakhs

EBITDA margin expanded 317 bps YoY to 23.75% in Q4

Operating cash flow to PAT ratio was 1.19x (strong cash conversion)

Met revised margin outlook of 22-23% with actual 22.50% for FY26

EBITDA grew 59% YoY in Q4 to ₹3,002.80 lakhs

Risk Factors

Cost of materials grew 42.5% YoY in Q4, outpacing revenue growth

Depreciation increased 97% YoY in Q4 due to capex

Free cash flow negative at ₹524.91 lakhs due to high capex

GST demand of ₹7.19 Cr and income tax demand of ₹41.76 Cr remain outstanding regulatory risks

items.title: Hero Motocorp delivered strong Q4 with 30.2% YoY revenue growth and 25.7% PAT growth, meeting its export growth target of >40% (achieved 40.2%) and ma...
items.description_html:
Hero Motocorp delivered strong Q4 with 30.2% YoY revenue growth and 25.7% PAT growth, meeting its export growth target of >40% (achieved 40.2%) and maintaining EBITDA margins within 14-16% guidance. EV market share nearly doubled to 11.2% and cash generation was exceptional with FCF of ₹7,215 crore.

The stock is well-positioned with a net cash position of ₹14,607 crore, though investors should note the one-time ₹736 crore gain inflating reported PAT.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 30.2% YoY to ₹12,978.28 crore in Q4
PAT grew 25.7% YoY to ₹1,460 crore (owners)

Export volumes grew 40.2% YoY meeting management's >40% target

EV market share nearly doubled from 6.1% to 11.2% YoY

Operating cash flow to PAT ratio strong at 1.44x

Free cash flow was ₹7,214.52 crore

Net cash position increased 36.5% to ₹14,606.54 crore

EBITDA margin expanded 33 bps to 14.86% for full year

Risk Factors

One-time gain of ₹735.81 crore inflated PAT; normalized PAT growth is 20.2%

Raw material costs as % of revenue increased 82 bps YoY to 65.6%

Associate operating performance was negative at -₹351.23 crore (excluding one-time gain)

Global business at 6.2% of volumes vs 10% long-term target

items.title: UBL reported mixed Q4 FY26 results with EBITDA growth driven by gross margin expansion and exceptional land gain, but underlying operating performance...
items.description_html:
UBL reported mixed Q4 FY26 results with EBITDA growth driven by gross margin expansion and exceptional land gain, but underlying operating performance deteriorated significantly with pre-exceptional PBT down 68% YoY.

The company faces severe near-term headwinds including a flagged cost inflation of ₹400-500 crore and sustained competitive pressure compressing EBIT margins to just 3.2%. While premiumization continues and working capital improved, the FY27 target of 10-11% EBIT margin appears highly challenging given current trajectory.

Positive Factors

EBITDA grew 14.2% YoY to ₹21,322 Lakhs with margins expanding 144 bps to 9.48%

Gross margin expanded 329 bps YoY to 45.5% in Q4, structural improvement confirmed

Operating cash flow more than doubled to ₹43,495 Lakhs (103.7% YoY)

Premium segment grew 21% in FY26 and 16% in Q4, outgrowing portfolio

Volume grew 4.1% YoY in Q4, recovering from weak Q2/Q3

Working capital improved significantly with CFO up 60.2% YoY

Risk Factors

Pre-exceptional PBT declined 68% YoY, normalized PAT declined 68.3% YoY

Q4 EBIT margin contracted to only 3.2% from 5.9% last year

Net revenue declined 3.1% YoY due to source mix and lower realizations

Finance costs surged 393.9% YoY due to higher borrowings

Capex heavy cycle: negative FCF of ₹7,677 Lakhs, funded by doubling of borrowings

Management flagged ₹400-500 crore cost headwind for next 2-3 quarters

Competitive intensity pressuring margins despite better gross margins

items.title: GALLANTT delivered strong Q4 revenue growth of 12.37% YoY but earnings growth lagged at 5.61% due to severe margin pressure from raw material cost inf...
items.description_html:
GALLANTT delivered strong Q4 revenue growth of 12.37% YoY but earnings growth lagged at 5.61% due to severe margin pressure from raw material cost inflation (23.6% YoY) and surging finance costs (151.9% YoY). While full-year performance showed operating leverage (PAT +20.8% vs revenue +2.9%), Q4 margin contraction of 178 bps is concerning.

The company maintains strong cash generation (FCF ₹254 Cr) and healthy balance sheet (D/E 0.16x), but working capital deterioration and debt buildup for working capital needs warrant monitoring.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 12.37% YoY to ₹1,204.81 Cr in Q4

Operating cash flow exceeded PAT (1.24x conversion ratio)

Free cash flow was ₹254.11 Cr, funding capex internally

Pellet production grew 30% and sponge iron grew 16.3% in 9M FY26

Full-year PAT grew 20.8% vs 2.9% revenue growth showing operating leverage

Cash and bank balances increased 1809% YoY to ₹503.15 Cr

Debt-to-equity ratio remains healthy at 0.16x

Risk Factors

PAT grew only 5.61% YoY vs 12.37% revenue growth - earnings lagging revenue

EBITDA margin contracted 178 bps YoY to 15.30% in Q4

Raw material costs surged 23.6% YoY outpacing revenue growth

Finance costs almost trebled (151.9% YoY) to ₹13.72 Cr

Trade receivables grew 96.9% YoY indicating earnings quality concern

Total debt increased 47% YoY to fund working capital

items.title: LLOYSME delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 results with revenue growth of 404% YoY and PAT growth of 603% YoY, significantly exceeding management's FY26 re...
items.description_html:
LLOYSME delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 results with revenue growth of 404% YoY and PAT growth of 603% YoY, significantly exceeding management's FY26 revenue guidance of ₹7,500 Cr with actuals at ₹17,113 Cr. EBITDA margin expanded 610 bps to 36% demonstrating strong operating leverage. The new MDO segment contributed 38% of consolidated revenue, while the steel segment's margin expanded 1300 bps to 30.3%.

However, the company faces elevated financial risk with debt increasing 19.7x to ₹20,380 Cr and liquidity metrics weakening (Current ratio 0.81x, DSCR 0.94x). FY27 guidance appears conservative and requires clarification.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 404.46% YoY to ₹6,019.72 Cr in Q4, FY26 revenue at ₹17,112.67 Cr (+154.61% YoY)

PAT to shareholders grew 603.13% YoY to ₹1,419.50 Cr in Q4, FY26 PAT at ₹3,680.85 Cr (+152.94% YoY)

EBITDA margin expanded 610 bps to 36.0% in FY26 (from 29.9% in FY25)

Operating leverage visible - Mining/Freight costs grew 77.8% vs revenue growth of 154.6%

MDO segment contributed ₹6,571.58 Cr revenue (38% of FY26 consolidated revenue)

Steel segment EBIT margin expanded from 16.4% to 30.3% (1300 bps improvement)

Risk Factors

Total debt increased 19.7x to ₹20,380.43 Cr from ₹1,033.84 Cr, Debt-to-Equity at 1.43x

Current ratio deteriorated to 0.81x from 1.51x, indicating liquidity pressure

Debt Service Coverage Ratio fell to 0.94x, below 1x threshold

Finance costs increased 18x to ₹510.36 Cr due to debt-funded capex and acquisitions

Working capital deteriorated - Inventory turnover days increased from 169 to 222

FY27 guidance appears conservative - revenue guidance of ₹10,000+ Cr vs FY26 actual ₹17,113 Cr

items.title: LT delivered strong top-line performance with revenue exceeding guidance and record order inflow providing multi-year revenue visibility. Cash generat...
items.description_html:
LT delivered strong top-line performance with revenue exceeding guidance and record order inflow providing multi-year revenue visibility. Cash generation was exceptional with FCF of ₹11,932 Crore and debt reduction.

However, profitability targets (ROE and margins) were missed. Strategic divestments of non-core assets (Nabha Power, Hyderabad Metro) are progressing. The company is transitioning from Lakshya 2026 to Lakshya 31 plan.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 11.8% YoY to ₹2.86 trillion, exceeding ₹2.7 trillion guidance

Record order inflow of ₹4,35,590 Crore (+22% YoY) with order book at ₹7,40,327 Crore (+28% YoY)

Operating cash flow surged 82.9% YoY to ₹16,741 Crore

Free cash flow strong at ₹11,932 Crore

Debt-equity ratio improved to 0.95x from 1.12x

IT & Technology Services segment turnaround: 11.3% revenue growth, 16% EBIT margin

Normalized PAT grew 18.2% YoY to ₹17,356 Crore

Dividend increased to ₹38/share from ₹34

items.title: SHILCTECH reported a significant revenue miss in Q4 FY26 with revenue declining 34.6% YoY, causing the full-year revenue of ₹651.94 Cr to fall ~13% b...
items.description_html:
SHILCTECH reported a significant revenue miss in Q4 FY26 with revenue declining 34.6% YoY, causing the full-year revenue of ₹651.94 Cr to fall ~13% below management's guidance of ₹750-800 Cr. However, the full-year story is mixed: EBITDA margin expanded 598 bps to 29.21%, cash conversion was excellent at 1.21x, and working capital improved dramatically.

The Q4 collapse suggests material demand headwinds (likely US tariff impact) that offset strong margin execution in the first three quarters. Balance sheet remains robust with 41.5% net worth growth and virtually debt-free status.

Positive Factors

Full-year EBITDA margin expanded 598 bps to 29.21% from 23.23%

Operating cash flow to PAT ratio strong at 1.21x

Working capital significantly improved: receivables down 32.8% YoY, payables down 34.3% YoY

Net worth grew 41.5% YoY to ₹49,084.60 Lakhs

Virtually debt-free balance sheet

Free cash flow strong at ₹16,892.45 Lakhs

Full-year PAT grew 7.7% YoY to ₹15,816.11 Lakhs despite revenue miss

Risk Factors

Q4 revenue collapsed 34.6% YoY, missing full-year guidance by ~13%

Q4 EBITDA margin collapsed 979 bps to 21.0% due to negative operating leverage

Q4 PAT declined 48.7% YoY

Management's revenue guidance of ₹750-800 Cr significantly missed at ₹651.94 Cr

US tariff impact and export headwinds materializing

Currency impact: unrealized forex loss of ₹346.15 Lakhs vs ₹67.98 Lakhs prior year

items.title: Shoppers Stop delivered mixed Q4 FY26 results with strong top-line growth (13.7% YoY) exceeding revenue guidance but profitability deteriorated with a...
items.description_html:
Shoppers Stop delivered mixed Q4 FY26 results with strong top-line growth (13.7% YoY) exceeding revenue guidance but profitability deteriorated with a net loss of ₹16.35 Cr. FY26 revenue of ₹5,043 Cr beat guidance (4-5% target) but Non-GAAP EBITDA margin of ~3.6% missed the mid-single digit target.

Key positives include strong cash generation (FCF ₹699 Cr) and debt reduction (30.6% YoY), while concerns center on the profit-to-loss transition and INTUNE delay. FY27 guidance targets 12-15% revenue growth and 50%+ reduction in INTUNE losses.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 9.0% YoY to ₹5,043.32 Cr, exceeding management guidance of 4-5% growth

Working capital reduced by ₹160 Cr, beating the ₹100 Cr target

Total borrowings (ex-lease) reduced 30.6% YoY to ₹235.77 Cr

Free cash flow strong at ₹698.93 Cr, up 91% YoY

Operating cash flow to EBITDA ratio healthy at 1.15x

Subsidiary Global SS Beauty Brands contributed 6.7% of revenue and helped reduce consolidated losses

Risk Factors

Q4 FY26 net loss of ₹16.35 Cr vs profit of ₹1.99 Cr in Q4 FY25 - profit to loss transition

FY26 Non-GAAP EBITDA margin ~3.6% missed guidance of mid-single digits (~5%)

Full year normalized loss of ₹20.54 Cr vs profit of ₹9.21 Cr in FY25

INTUNE breakeven delayed to FY27 from end FY26
Expense growth (14.0% YoY) outpaced revenue growth (13.7% YoY) in Q4

Sequential revenue declined 14.6% QoQ post festive season

items.title: AAVAS delivered a solid Q4 with PAT growth of 18.2% YoY, beating its own guidance on credit costs (19.5 bps vs <25 bps target) and GNPA (1.05% vs 1.07...
items.description_html:
AAVAS delivered a solid Q4 with PAT growth of 18.2% YoY, beating its own guidance on credit costs (19.5 bps vs <25 bps target) and GNPA (1.05% vs 1.07% guidance). Asset quality improved markedly with GNPA declining from 1.19% in Q3 to 1.05%.

However, loan book growth decelerated to 13.2% YoY from 15-18% prior levels, and the FY27 guidance of 17-18% loan growth requires a significant 46-54% acceleration which poses execution risk. The company remains well-capitalized with CRAR at 44.56%.

Positive Factors

NII grew 17.3% YoY to ₹11,849.43 Cr, with Q4 NII growth accelerating to 18.3% YoY

PAT grew 14.1% YoY to ₹654.88 Cr; Q4 PAT grew faster at 18.2% YoY

GNPA improved to 1.05% from 1.19% in Q3; NNPA improved to 0.68% from 0.79%

Credit costs for FY26 were 19.5 bps, within management's guidance of below 25 bps

Q4 disbursements grew 36% QoQ showing strong momentum

CRAR remains very robust at 44.56%, well above regulatory requirements

Added 38 branches in Q4 alone, accelerating network expansion

Risk Factors

Loan book growth moderated to 13.2% YoY from 15-18% in prior quarters

Opex growth of 20.6% YoY outpaced Net Operating Income growth of 17.8%

Cost-to-income ratio increased to 46.22% in Q4 from 42.9% in Q3

FY27 loan book growth guidance of 17-18% requires 46-54% acceleration from FY26 baseline

FY27 disbursement growth target of 25%+ vs current 16% YoY poses execution challenge

items.title: Spandana returned to profitability in Q4 FY26 with PAT of ₹5.27 cr driven by impairment reversal, marking a clear turnaround after 4 quarters of loss...
items.description_html:
Spandana returned to profitability in Q4 FY26 with PAT of ₹5.27 cr driven by impairment reversal, marking a clear turnaround after 4 quarters of losses. Asset quality improved significantly with GNPA at 3.33% and strong capital adequacy at 29.76%.

However, the full year still recorded a net loss of ₹699 cr and revenue declined 33.84% YoY due to deliberate loan book cleanup. FY27 guidance targets 20% AUM growth from a significantly contracted base.

Positive Factors

PAT turned positive at ₹5.27 cr in Q4 FY26 vs loss of ₹434.30 cr in Q4 FY25

PPOP sequential recovery of 178% QoQ from ₹41.73 cr to ₹115.99 cr

Impairment reversal of ₹18.06 cr vs charge of ₹602.76 cr in Q4 FY25

GNPA improved to 3.33% from 4.97% in Q2 FY26

CRAR strong at 29.76% well above regulatory minimum
PCR robust at 80.62%

Employee costs down 29.8% YoY demonstrating cost control

Total debt reduced 26.4% YoY to ₹3,824.25 cr

Liquidity Coverage Ratio strong at 285%

Risk Factors

Revenue declined 33.84% YoY due to loan book contraction

NII declined 48.5% YoY reflecting smaller loan book

Full year FY26 net loss of ₹699.09 cr despite Q4 profit

AUM shrank 32.4% YoY missing 20% growth target massively

Net worth eroded 16.4% YoY to ₹2,193.75 cr

FY27 AUM growth target of 20% from very low base may be ambitious

items.title: Shankara Building Products achieved a turnaround in FY26, turning from loss to profit with PAT of ₹3.84 Cr. The company demonstrated significant marg...
items.description_html:
Shankara Building Products achieved a turnaround in FY26, turning from loss to profit with PAT of ₹3.84 Cr. The company demonstrated significant margin expansion, particularly in Q4 where EBITDA margin reached 3.87%, on track to meet the FY27 target of 3%.

However, severe cash flow deterioration (negative CFO of ₹101 Cr) and working capital stress (receivables and inventories up significantly) funded by 300% increase in borrowings present key risks to monitor.

Positive Factors

Q4 FY26 PAT turned positive at ₹7.35 Cr vs loss of ₹0.94 Cr in Q4 FY25

Q4 FY26 EBITDA margin expanded to 3.87% from 1.38% in Q4 FY25 (249 bps improvement)

FY26 PAT turned positive at ₹3.84 Cr vs loss of ₹0.79 Cr in FY25

Cost of materials as % of revenue fell to 86.7% in Q4 FY26 from 99.7% in Q4 FY25

Q4 EBITDA grew 192.2% YoY to ₹13.18 Cr

FY26 EBITDA grew 27.3% YoY to ₹23.48 Cr

Risk Factors

Operating cash flow severely negative at ₹(100.94) Cr for FY26

Working capital deteriorated significantly - inventories up 27.2%, receivables up 96.26%

Current borrowings surged 303.75% to fund working capital and capex

Free cash flow deeply negative at ₹(165.98) Cr

Other expenses rose 35.9% YoY, growing faster than revenue

Finance costs increased 38.2% YoY indicating higher borrowings

items.title: Mafatlal Industries reported strong revenue recovery in Q4 with 96.47% YoY growth to ₹883.70 Cr, recovering from Q3 decline. However, PAT declined 22...
items.description_html:
Mafatlal Industries reported strong revenue recovery in Q4 with 96.47% YoY growth to ₹883.70 Cr, recovering from Q3 decline. However, PAT declined 22.81% as margin compression of 113 bps YoY offset the revenue surge.

The Consumer Durables segment drove growth but at very thin margins of 1.83%, while Digital Infrastructure continued to be a drag with 65.5% EBIT collapse. Operating cash flow turned strongly positive at ₹140.64 Cr, indicating improved cash generation despite profit decline. No specific management guidance was provided for FY26.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 96.47% YoY to ₹883.70 Cr in Q4, recovering sharply from Q3 decline

FY26 revenue grew 37.9% YoY to ₹3,871.07 Cr, outperforming prior year

Operating cash flow turned strongly positive at ₹140.64 Cr vs outflow of ₹83.86 Cr in FY25

Gross debt reduced from ₹68.29 Cr to ₹60.77 Cr YoY, continuing deleveraging

Consumer Durables segment revenue surged 264% YoY in Q4, becoming new growth engine

Cash and cash equivalents doubled to ₹123.94 Cr from ₹51.19 Cr YoY

Risk Factors

PAT declined 22.81% YoY to ₹17.94 Cr in Q4 despite revenue surge - earnings lagging revenue

Segment EBIT margin contracted 113 bps YoY to 2.02% in Q4 due to low-margin business mix

Digital Infrastructure segment revenue fell 32.9% YoY and EBIT collapsed 65.5%

Consumer Durables EBIT margin wafer-thin at 1.83% in Q4, growth not translating to profits

Working capital expanded significantly with receivables growing faster than revenue

Full-year PAT declined 9.05% to ₹89.07 Cr despite revenue growth

items.title: Freshworks CEO Dennis Woodside on why it's laying off over 500 employees: "Over half of our code is originated in AI today....that is definitely chang...
items.description_html:
Freshworks CEO Dennis Woodside on why it's laying off over 500 employees:

"Over half of our code is originated in AI today....that is definitely changing the amount of people we need to build product."

The other reason is restructuring the sales team to focus on EX, or IT service management biz (Freshservice), which is growing at 27% YoY. This will account for 60% of revenues by year's end.

The original CX (Freshdesk) business is now growing at just 6% YoY and will continue in "low single digits."


The Arc: 🚨 Freshworks has decided to lay off 11% of its workforce, or about 500 employees.

This is the second major layoff at the Chennai-based software firm in the last two years - in Nov 2024, it fired 660 employees, or 13% of the workforce.

Freshworks reported a 16% rise in revenues

items.title: RT Gary Marcus: The case against OpenAI is getting markedly stronger now that Musk is off the stand. Why? Musk’s lawyer is interrogating OpenAI found...
items.description_html:
RT Gary Marcus
The case against OpenAI is getting markedly stronger now that Musk is off the stand.

Why?

Musk’s lawyer is interrogating OpenAI founder Greg Brockman, making clear that OpenAI sold its mission as a nonprofit for public benefit, and not just to Elon but to many big figures in Silicon Valley, seeking *donations* to establish the company as a nonprofit.

This case is *not about Elon*. It’s about the bait and switch that OpenAI pulled, raising money as a nonprofit and then switching after ChatGPT hit bit.

Yes, the company misled Elon, but also misled many many others, from the state of California to other early “donors”, to early employees and the public.

The question is whether that is ok.

items.title: Milky Mist Dairy Food, a largely bootstrapped 30-year-old company, has been valued post-money at over Rs 9,300 crore or close to $1 billion. It has ra...
items.description_html:
Milky Mist Dairy Food, a largely bootstrapped 30-year-old company, has been valued post-money at over Rs 9,300 crore or close to $1 billion.

It has raised Rs 482 crore in a pre-IPO round from Singapore’s Temasek, benchmarking its valuation ahead of the IPO.

The transaction includes a primary infusion of Rs 357 crore and a secondary sale of Rs 125 crore by founder-promoters Sathish Kumar T and Anitha S.

The promoters will continue to hold a 90% stake after this fundraising.
